figures.caption: Figure 1: Safe continual reinforcement learning benchmarks. Top: HalfCheetah MuJoCo task with a maximum velocity constraint. Every 1 million timesteps, the cheetah is damaged or repaired, significantly changing its dynamics. Middle: Ant MuJoCo task with a maximum velocity constraint. Every 1 million timesteps, the ant is damaged or repaired, significantly changing its dynamics. Bottom: three tasks from the Safe Continual World benchmark. A coffee mug is placed in the scene, and the agent is penalized for tipping the mug while solving the task. Every 2 million timesteps, the task changes, and the task sequence is repeated once.
Figure A.14: Reward training curves for 5 seeds of all algorithms on Damaged Ant Velocity.
Figure A.15: Cost training curves for 5 seeds of all algorithms on Damaged Ant Velocity.

Figure 4: Behaviors during learning. Top: Reward behavior during training of two safe RL algorithms (CPO and CPPO-PID) versus a safe continual RL algorithm (Safe EWC) on the Damaged HalfCheetah Velocity environment. Middle: Reward behavior during training of a safe RL algorithm (CPO) versus a safe and continual RL algorithm (Safe EWC) on the Damaged Ant Velocity environment. Bottom: Cost behavior during training of a continual (PPO+EWC) versus a safe continual RL algorithm (Safe EWC).
Table A.11: Safe Continual World results broken down by task across 5 random seeds.
Table A.10: Damaged Ant Velocity results broken down by task across 5 random seeds.
Figure A.17: Cost training curves for all algorithms on Safe Continual World.
Figure A.16: Success curves during training all algorithms on Safe Continual World.
Figure A.9: Cost curves for different algorithms across 5 random seeds on Damaged Ant Velocity task.

Figure 3: Average safe continual RL metrics for the algorithms on each environment. Algorithms that better handle the tradeoff among safety, reward/success, and forgetting will be higher and closer to the front of the plot. Cost is shown on a log scale.
Figure A.8: Reward curves for different algorithms across 5 random seeds on Damaged Ant Velocity task.
Figure A.10: Success curves for different algorithms on Safe Continual World task.
Table A.9: Damaged HalfCheetah Velocity results broken down by task across 5 random seeds.
Table 3: Safe Continual World results. Mean ± standard deviation across 5 random seeds and all tasks.
Table 2: Damaged Ant Velocity results. Mean ± standard deviation across 5 random seeds and all tasks.
Figure A.11: Cost curves for different algorithms on Safe Continual World task.
Table 5: Ablation building up from the core components of CF-EWC (PPO+EWC+Cost Fisher) and Safe EWC (PPO+EWC+Safe Reward). Mean ± standard deviation across 5 random seeds and all tasks.
Figure A.7: Cost curves for different algorithms across 5 random seeds on Damaged HalfCheetah Velocity task.
Table 1: Damaged HalfCheetah Velocity results. Mean ± standard deviation across 5 random seeds and all tasks.

Table A.8: Selected Safe Continual World hyperparameters. * indicates they were chosen through hyperparameter optimization. Others were kept consistent with standard implementations. Safe EWC used PPO+EWC’s hyperparameters with the cost weight. CF-EWC used PPO+EWC’s hyperparameters, as there were no new hyperparameters associated with this algorithm.
Figure A.12: Reward training curves for 5 seeds of all algorithms on Damaged HalfCheetah Velocity.
Figure A.13: Cost training curves for 5 seeds of all algorithms on Damaged HalfCheetah Velocity.
Figure A.6: Reward curves for different algorithms across 5 random seeds on Damaged HalfCheetah Velocity task.

Figure 5: Cost around the moment the task changes in Safe Continual World with a safe (CPO) and safe continual (Safe EWC) algorithm. The task change triggers safety violations in CPO, which is achieving a higher reward.
